title: The basics of investing
description: Plant money today, pick fruit for decades — starting with $5.
summaryPoints: Investing puts money to work; spending makes it vanish
Stocks = owning, bonds = lending, funds = a basket of both
Stocks return about 7–10% a year on average — with big swings
Starting at 18 instead of 30 can roughly double your final pot
Emergency fund first, then small automatic investments

content.title: Plant it, don't just park it
content.body:
Spend $10 on a game skin and it's gone forever. Invest that $10 and it has a shot at becoming $15, $20, or more — because investing means using money today to grow more money tomorrow.

Think of it like planting a tree. You bury a seed (your cash), water it (add a little regularly), and wait. Years later it's dropping fruit — money your money made — while you do absolutely nothing.
content.imageAlt: A money tree growing from a planted seed

content.title: Why not just save?
content.body:
Savings accounts keep money safe, but barely grow it:
- $100 saved at 1% interest → $101 after a year
- $100 invested in the stock market → historically around 7–10% a year ON AVERAGE, so maybe $108

The catch: "on average" hides wild swings. Some years stocks jump 20%, some years they drop 20%. Investing trades a smooth ride for a faster one — which is why it's for money you won't need soon.

content.prompt: Investing in the stock market is basically gambling.
content.explanation: Gambling is a bet where the odds are against you. Owning diversified investments for years means owning real businesses that grow — though betting everything on one hot stock IS closer to gambling.

content.prompt: Why invest at all when savings accounts are safer?
options: Investing is guaranteed to beat savings
Banks charge fees on savings accounts
Investing has much higher growth potential over time
content.explanation: No guarantees — markets swing — but historically investments have grown far faster than savings. You're trading some safety for growth.

content.title: The big three
content.body:
Almost every investment you'll meet is one of these:
- **Stocks** — you own a tiny slice of a company. Nike does well, your slice is worth more. Some even pay you a cut of profits (dividends).
- **Bonds** — you LEND money to a company or government, and they pay you back with interest. Slower, steadier.
- **Mutual funds & ETFs** — baskets holding many stocks and bonds at once, so one company's bad year can't wreck you.

content.prompt: Match each investing word to what it really is.

pairs.right: Owning a slice of a company
A loan you give, repaid with interest
Profit a company shares with you

content.prompt: A $1,000 government bond pays 3% simple yearly interest. After 5 years you collect $___ in total.
content.hint: That's $30 a year, five times, plus your $1,000 back.
content.explanation: $1,000 × 3% = $30 a year. Five years = $150 in interest, plus your original $1,000 back = $1,150.

content.title: Risk and reward: a package deal
content.body:
There's no prize without a price:
- **High risk, high potential reward** — individual stocks, crypto. Could double, could halve.
- **Low risk, low reward** — government bonds, savings. Slow, steady, boring.

The pro move isn't picking one extreme — it's **diversifying**: spreading money across different investments so one flop can't take you down. That's exactly what funds do for you automatically.

content.prompt: What's the smart move for long-term money?
content.context: Three months after you start investing, the market drops 15%. Your $500 is now $425.
options: Sell everything before it falls further
Stay invested — markets have recovered from every crash so far
Move it all into crypto to win it back fast
Sell now, wait for the bottom, buy back in
content.explanation: Selling in a panic locks in the loss. Historically, investors who stayed put rode the recovery — and timing the bottom almost never works.

content.title: Time is your superpower
content.body:
Compound growth means your earnings start earning. It's a snowball rolling downhill: tiny at first, unstoppable later.

$50 a month at about 7% average growth:
- Start at 18 → roughly $230,000 by 65
- Start at 30 → roughly $100,000 by 65

Same habit, same monthly amount — starting 12 years earlier more than doubles the pot. The biggest investing advantage you will ever have is being young.

content.prompt: You invest $100 once at 7% average growth and ignore it for 30 years. Slide to where it lands.
content.explanation: At 7%, money doubles roughly every 10 years: $100 → $200 → $400 → about $760 after 30. The last decade earns more than the first two combined.

content.instructions: One word turns this into terrible advice. Tap it.
content.sentence: Putting all your eggs in one basket is the safest way to invest.
content.explanation: One basket means one bad drop breaks everything. Diversification exists so a single flop can't wipe you out.

content.title: Starting with pocket change
content.body:
You don't need $10,000 — you need a habit:
- **Micro-investing apps** let you start with $5–$10
- **Low-cost index funds and ETFs** buy you the whole market in one move
- **Automate it** — small and consistent beats big and occasional

Two rules before your first dollar goes in: build a small emergency fund FIRST (so a surprise bill never forces you to sell), and treat anything promising fast guaranteed riches as a scam — because it is.

content.prompt: You should invest your emergency fund so it grows instead of sitting in savings.
content.explanation: Emergency money must be there the day you need it. If a crash and a car repair hit the same week, an invested emergency fund forces you to sell at a loss.

content.title: The $1,000 Year
content.intro: You've got $1,000 and 12 months. Beat the market's mood swings and end the year ahead.

steps.situation: January. Time to put your $1,000 to work. How do you spread it?

options.text: All-in on one hot tech stock
options.feedback: It spiked, then sank. One company means one bad headline can sink you.

options.text: Split it: stocks, bonds, and an index fund
options.feedback: Diversified. No single flop can take you out — and the mix still grows.

options.text: Keep it all in cash for now
options.feedback: Zero risk, zero growth — and inflation quietly shrank what your cash can buy.

steps.situation: April. Tech boom! The stock slice of your portfolio jumps 20%.

options.text: Sell everything and lock in the win
options.feedback: You banked a small win, but now you're out of the market — and re-entering is scary.

options.text: Hold your plan, trim just a little profit
options.feedback: You stayed invested for the growth and skimmed a bit of profit. Balanced.

options.text: Move everything into tech — it's clearly winning
options.feedback: Chasing a spike is how people buy at the top. It cooled off right after.

steps.situation: August. Market crash — stocks plunge 15% in a week and your feed is pure panic.

options.text: Sell it all before it gets worse
options.feedback: You locked in the loss. The market recovered within months; your sold shares didn't.

options.text: Stay invested, keep monthly contributions going
options.feedback: Every crash so far has eventually recovered — and you bought the dip on autopilot.

options.text: Stop looking AND stop contributing for a while
options.feedback: Not selling helps, but pausing contributions means skipping the discount aisle.

steps.situation: October. Interest rates rise: bonds now pay more, and stocks dip 5%.

options.feedback: Overcorrection. You traded long-term growth for a small bump in safety.

options.text: Nudge a little more into bonds, keep the mix
options.feedback: A light rebalance keeps your plan on target without abandoning growth.
Doing nothing beats panicking, but a small rebalance would've kept your mix on plan.

steps.situation: December. Your car needs a $200 repair — this can't wait.

options.text: Sell stocks (still recovering from the crash)
options.feedback: You sold low and turned the crash into a real, permanent loss. Ouch.
Safer assets are your shock absorber — stable value, no fire sale required.

options.text: Credit card at 24% APR, leave investments alone
options.feedback: That interest costs more than your investments earn. The math loses every time.

content.outro: Finish above $1,000 and you beat a chaotic year. The winning moves were boring on purpose: diversify, hold through the panic, rebalance lightly, and let bonds absorb the emergencies.
